speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Iona Adair took place at our home in Moscow on February 24th, 1977. Her sister, Bernadine Adair Cornelison, also took part in the conversation. The interviewer was Sam Schrager.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: I'm wondering why there were so many women here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why the women went off there?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Well, in the first place, the locator out through that part of the country had a wife that wanted a timber claim, a sister that wanted a timber claim, two sisters that wanted timber claims, and there was a couple of women here in town that wanted timber claims that we all knew. So, when the locator had located this particular batch of timber out in what was called the 49 Meadow Country, and he took this bunch out to show them the country and the timber. And if they wanted to locate, then we could all locate there within a certain distance of each other. It was practically a little colony by themselves. So, that's the way we all happened to go out there. And I joined the bunch of Mrs. Torsen and Mrs. Durham and Mrs. Taylor and Winifred Calkins and Kip Calkins.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Following us when we located out there was Erwin Hansen and Art Larson went out with him just to be on the homestead with him so he'd have company. And they were on one side. And they were four miles and a half off on one side. And Mrs. Torsen was in three quarters of a mile. That was a colony. Mrs. Durham. And my homestead was about four miles over on the other side. And Mrs. Taylor's was over that way. And so we all got together just as a group and would take our trips out together. And it was a saving on packers and so forth when we all went together. So, that was...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What else? Were most of these ladies as self -reliant as you were? These women?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I can't hear you. Well, it seemed like to be a homesteader it took a lot of self -reliance. You had to depend on yourself and your own resources.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: You had to depend upon yourself. But the others, of course, surrounding you that were homesteaders, or that took up homesteaders, all helped if you needed any help or anything like that. And Mrs. Durham and I planned the winter together. I was four miles and a half from her place. And so we decided that she and her daughter and I would live together that winter. And I would go back and forth to my place as I wished. And it was all snowshoeing, of course, mostly. And that's the way we traveled. Then my father was afraid that something might happen and that we'd have no means of getting word out to the outside of an emergency. So he and Mrs. Durham and I decided that we'd hire a man to stay with us that winter. And so my father made the arrangements with a man by the name of Carter. And Bill Carter said that he would trap that winter and would try to make up for any additional expenses that he would do or needed. And that he would go out once a month anyway to get the mail and bring it to us. And the rest of the time he would spend trapping in that part of the country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he staying at the same cabin that you and...

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Mrs. Durham and her daughter and I, we were the three that were together. And Mrs. Turson, who was a miller here in town, had a cabin about three -quarters of a mile from our place. But she only spent the summers out there. Let her sister run the business here in town and she went out and spent it on the homestead while the weather was good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, I was just wondering, did he, when he was there, did he have to sleep in the barn or was there...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could he sleep in the house?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There was no barn there. He could sleep in the house too then. He slept in the house with us, yes.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: We had, I don't know whether you know, beared grass or not. But it's a very wiry grass and holds up when you make a mattress of it. And we made a full -size mattress for him. He was over six feet tall. We made a full -size mattress for him of this. And each day we would roll it up, put a blanket over it, and it could be used as a seat. In case that you wanted extra accommodations.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just a mat of bare grass? It was just a mat of bare grass?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Just a mattress of bare grass. We made the mattress out of gunny sacks that we had and then cut the bare grass and laid the mattress for him. And that was his bedroom. And we had a bedroom across the end of the cabin. And I had a single bed and Mrs. Durham and her daughter had a double bed. And during the daytime we had fixed them so they would, the double bed would fold up in the wall and out of the way. And the single bed was used as a seat during the daytime.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Didn't you have an attic in that place a second start?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: We had an attic in the place. And that's where we kept flour and sugar or things that the moisture might affect. We had an outdoor cellar and we kept the canned goods in this outdoor cellar. But the upper was the attic. We kept the flour and the sugar because that would be warm from the heat in the cabin. And it wouldn't get solid.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Carter one day got everything all ready to go out for the mail. And left early in the morning for it was, they called it 28 miles out to Avery by trail. And he didn't go back in to Clarkia because that was a longer route. It took 45 miles to get in that way. So he went out the short way to Avery and the mail was all sent to Avery. And he would go out and get it. Well he got everything all ready and left early. And after he left Mrs. Durham decided that she had to wash. And the extra soap and stuff she needed was up in the attic. And the only way into the attic was by ladder and the trap door. So we brought in the ladder and opened the trap door. And Mrs. Durham went up to the attic to get the flour or whatever it was that she needed. And about the time she got up there the ice on the legs of the ladder broke off and the ladder went down. Mrs. Durham went with it. And as she went down she hit the end of her spine on the ladder and dropped off in a faint. Well Myrtle and I didn't know what to do so we just grabbed up a bottle of camphor that was there. And intended to put it under her nose where she'd breathe it. And Myrtle miscalculated and put it on her eye. On her forehead and it run down into her eye. Well that brought Mrs. Durham to in one grand rush. We grabbed her while she was in that condition and put her over on my bed. And she lay there all afternoon just moaning, oh my tailbone, oh my tailbone. It nearly drove us crazy because we didn't know what to do about it until Carter got back anyway. We couldn't send word out. We couldn't do anything. And neither one of us were going off and leaving the other one alone with the mother on a 28 mile trip out to town. So we just endured moaning and tried to keep her as comfortable as possible. When Carter got back then he could see that Mrs. Durham was in a very bad condition and would have to be taken off to town. And so we waited for about a week until she got to feeling a little better. And then Carter took her and started off to Avery as a 28 mile trip for a woman with her tailbone injured and on snowshoes. So she got along very nicely until about the last mile and a half or two miles of the trip. And then she broke down. She just couldn't go any farther. So Carter practically carried her on in to Avery. And then they got transportation from Avery on the train and brought her down to Tico and on into Moscow. So we had her in the hospital for quite a little while here after that until she began to get better. Well, Myrtle and I were then left alone with just Carter as companions. And so we got along very nicely. But the next time Carter went off to Avery to see how Mrs. Durham was getting along. And it left Myrtle and I alone. And we were sitting eating our breakfast one morning and the cabin door opened. And in walked a grizzly funny looking little old fellow. And he said, oh you're here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And we said, yes, who are you?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: He said, I'm dynamite.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I said, you are?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: He said, yes, they all call me dynamite. He said, I'm working on the railroads and on the tunnels. And I do the drilling for the holes and putting the dynamite in. And that's where I got my name.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I said, you do have another name, don't you?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: He said, yes, I'm Joel Chandler Harris. And he said, my mother thought the name of Joel Chandler sounded very nice. She'd been reading about him. And so she called me Joel Chandler Harris. Well, he came in and he stayed. He said, I'll stay here until your man gets back. So he stayed. We couldn't say get out. We had no means of putting him out or anything. And so we said, all right. So he took the bed and unrolled the bed that night. And that was his bed until Carter got back. Well, when Carter got back, then dynamite left and went over to Larson and Hansen's cabin. And he spent the greater part of the winter at the Larson and Hansen cabin. But he was a funny looking little fellow with whiskers all over.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he staying there?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: We were so surprised. He said, I've been living in your cabin while you were gone. We knew somebody had been there, but we had no idea who nor what. We said, I've been there. He said, when your man gets back, I'll go on over to the boys. And that's what he did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he staying there to protect you? Was that the idea? Was he staying there to protect you? Was that his thinking?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: We don't know. We don't know what his idea was. He was just looking for a place to land. And he saw the place was vacant and he just landed. So we did a lot of blanket washing and all when we got to capturing this.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, he stayed at Elsie Files' place too. Because I know Carol Brink once mentioned that to me.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: She mentioned this guy, Dynamite.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She spoke about Dynamite too, did she?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Well, Elsie was a class on what they called, another branch of the little north bird called the Floodwood. It flew, it went south and east. It went south and east and the potlatch went the other way. And we were on the other side of the ridge from Elsie. She was quite a character. You should have known her. She was delightful. She certainly was. One night we were going on the trip. My father, after I had been out there so long, the government decided I had to come into a trial at Wallace. And in order to get to the trial at Wallace, I had to be notified to get out there. And there was no one in this part of the country that knew where I was or the location or anything. And so my father undertook to come out and notify me, which he did. And he left word that we would need a packer to bring us out. And he walked out that 25 miles from Avery, 28 miles, and told me that I had to come in for the trip. And so they got the packer out and the packer, Ira McPeak, lived at Clarkia. And he had been packing things with us for us so that he knew where we were and how to get to us and so forth. And he brought the horses out for us. And he brought a horse out for me to ride that an Indian had owned. And he lost the race for the Indian. And the Indian was so mad that he cut the horse's tail off and his ears right close to his head. Well, the day that we started out with the pack train, after my father got there, they put me on Jim the horse. Bob.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Bob. He called the horse Bob. Bob because of his bob ears and bob tails. And McPeak said, you ride Bob because he is so sure of footing. And he said, you ride out on Bob. Well, I had taken cold a little while before and I was subject to attacks of Quincy. And so they dolled me up and everything they could find in the cabin. It was warm. And I mounted Bob and we started off. Well, there's one place that we used to call little Lake Thelma. It's a different name than that now on the maps. And I don't know what it's called now. But at any rate, you had to go up a very high, very steep ridge to get up to the top and come on over into the Tarkia country. And we always, in going up, hung onto our horse's tail and made the horse pull us up instead of riding because it was this way. It was so rough you couldn't ride comfortably on it. We always held onto the horse's tail. Well, I was on Bob, which had neither ears nor tail. And so I had to stay on the horse. And I'd been having the Quincy at the cabin and was not in a very good condition to be riding out there anyway. Well, we got to what was called the, not the company, it was the halfway cabin. It was the government cabin. And they had blankets and cooking utensils and heating and so forth at the cabin. We got to the cabin that night and who was at the cabin but Elsie Vile. She had heard that...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Anybody home?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Yes. I always give you a recording of you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did we come again? Did you understand me?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you met...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who was he?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: She was across the street. Oh, across the street.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So Elsie Vile was there?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Elsie Vile had heard through the, well I suppose it would be the wireless, that McPete was coming out to our place and she wanted to come out to Tarkia. So she walked from her place and down to this government cabin because she knew we'd stop there. And she had with her a great big bulldog. And see that was for protection on her cabin, that she had this big bulldog. Well she was at the cabin that night when we got in. And I had this enormous head of hair. And the rain had wet the neck of the turtleneck sweater I was wearing. And the sweater was wet. And we couldn't get out of the sweater. So McPete took one side, the packer took one side, Elsie took the other, and they took my hair down and the skin, this turtleneck sweater up over my head until they finally got it off and I was made as comfortable as possible. Well Elsie and I slept in one bed that night, just little bunk beds in the government cabin. And along in the night came the pack rats. And the bulldog didn't like pack rats. So he started chasing him all around the house, in the cabin, all around the floor. And whenever the pack rats would be, the dog would get too close, the pack rat would be up on the beds. And the dog up after him, you can imagine how much sleep went on in that cabin that night. We couldn't calm the dog down on account of the rats. And we couldn't catch the rats and he couldn't catch the rats.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Couldn't just kick him out of the cabin?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Couldn't chase him out. They didn't have any idea of going out of the cabin.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The dog?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Oh no, it was cold, snow deep outside. You don't put a dog out in the snow and leave him. Especially a short haired dog like a bulldog. But, we got off the next morning and went on in to Clarkia. Elsie took the dog and went with us. And we all got to Clarkia safe. And it was quite a trip that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your father had come out all that distance by snowshoe? Had he snowshoed out to the cabin?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Yes, snowshoes or walking. Which is amazing because he was not an athletic person, but determined.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you got to Clarkia, you still had a long ways to go to Wallace.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get on the train there?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Oh, I had a long ways to go. Yes, my father stayed there with me for two or three days until I got my throat cleared out. And then we went on up to Wallace and got there in time for the trial. The difficulty was that the railroad had placed, what do you say, entries, had filed on a lot of what was supposed to be railroad land in that section of the country. And they were given by the government certain townships and certain, what do you call it, railroads had rights over certain townships in order to settle up this part of the country. And some of those railroads, townships or filings, were over on my place and part of them were on Mrs. Cockin's place. So we all had to go in for the government hearing at Wallace. But we could take the train from Tekoa and make connections and get up to Wallace for the train.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you win that time?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You lost that?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: No, I lost it. I lived there for 22 months or more and fought the fire and fed the firemen with the help and made the trips back and forth. But I lost it. We did the best we could and Bertnell French was a representative from Idaho back at Washington, D .C. And Bertnell tried his best to have it reopened and a new van put on. But they said no, it wasn't. It was that I was over on the other, my location had gone over and that I hadn't lived out there at a sufficient length of time. They sent out one of these little, we call them patent leather men from Washington, D .C. and patent leather shoe men that didn't know a darn thing about timber or living in the woods or even living. And he'd come in, he'd look over your little cabin and say, it's not a suitable habitation. And I had a little garden patch and I raised radishes of lettuce out there and all. He looked at my garden patch and said it's not sufficient cultivation for a wood. And did that...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that to your face?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: To your face?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Yeah, oh yes. He didn't mind telling us. That went on like that. He came in and he got Mrs. Durham and Mrs. Torsen's place and my place and Kip Cockin's place next to mine where she was located on one end of the meadow and I was at the other end of the meadow. Our cabins were quite close so I had company out there and she lost hers and I don't know, there's a whole bunch of them in through there that lost it on account of this man from Washington, D .C. that didn't think people could live under those conditions. Although we were living under them. Well, during the fire season in 1910, this same man was put in charge of a firefighters' brigade that was sent off into that part of the country. And at that time, he had sent his report in to Washington, D .C. and they'd cancelled all of our homesteads out there but we were still on the homestead because we didn't know but what it might be opened or reopened at any time. You never know when they were fighting it. And he was put on in charge of the firefighting brigade that was sent off into that section of the country. And one morning, Mrs. Taylor, who lived alone about a mile and a half or two miles from Mrs. Cockin's place, and she was a little uneasy about the smoke that had been drifting into the country. So she came over to stay with me so as not to be alone at her cabin in case anything should happen. Well, we were... there was a rat at the door that was just barely outside and Mrs. Taylor got up and answered the door and there was this man who had reported all of these homesteaders standing at the door. And he said that he was in charge of a firefighting crew and that they were trying to locate the fire which was causing all the smoke in this part of the country. And he wanted to know if there was a trail any farther east. So she told him of her trail that went farther east and he said he'd take his crew and go over there. Well, we got up and dressed and decided that we'd walk over and see if they had found her cabin and if they were still there, which they were when we got there. And the men that were camping around the fire on the outside, some of them were inside of the cabin and were trying to get something for breakfast. And Mrs. Taylor said that she would help the men. So she got busy and fixed pancakes and things for the men inside the cabin and outside the cabin. And when they went to move on then to locate farther to locate the fire, Mrs. or Mr. Roach said to Mrs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Taylor, wouldn't you ladies like to go on with us a little farther and get a meal for us at noon so we'd have something to eat?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: And we said we would. And so we followed the night. We got them a lunch of pancakes or rolled cakes so they'd have something to eat at noon. And that evening then they wanted us to stay and get the evening meal for them. Well that was my first attempt at cooking outside over a fireplace and also Mrs. Taylor's first attempt. But we managed to get something for the men to eat that night. And they fixed a bed for us right along by the side of the horses that they had taken in there. And the horses kept stomping and chewing and stomping and trying to fight the flies all night long. So we didn't get very much sleep. But the next morning they wanted us to continue on and take care of the men we had.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many did you have?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: We had about ten or fifteen that we were trying to fix for them. And they had their own provisions but we had to take care of the provisions and the cooking. By the way, it took me years to like potatoes. I cooked so many potatoes in water buckets over the fire. I didn't like potatoes for a long, long time after that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you prepared to go on with them?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: You didn't have your clothing and all your belongings. No, we just learned what we had and got along with the time being. Because they had to keep on working and going far. They had a man.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was his name?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Johnny, Johnny, Johnny, Johnny. Anyway, he was a man here in town that we knew of that was a cook. And he had gone on the firefighting crew just for the experience and was a cook. And they left him up on the top of what was called the freeze out ridge when they camped there the night before coming on to our cabin. And they left him up there and he said that he would pack things and have everything all ready and that the crew coming through could pick them up. And that he'd cut across country and come over to this part of the country. But Johnny missed the packers. And Johnny missed everything when he started out across country and he got lost. And so he just kept traveling. He said he thought he'd find something sometime. And he finally landed in the camp. But for that time they needed firefighters so bad that Johnny wasn't allowed to cook. He was put on the firefighting. And Mrs. Durham and I continued cooking. And then they brought in a team of...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: what were they called?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: They were short termers. You told me about that. Yeah. From Montana. Right. And they had a camp right next to ours. But they had their own cook. And finally Mr. Roach gave Mrs. Taylor and me a man and a plunky to help us out so we didn't have quite so much work to do after that. But at any rate, Johnny was put on the firing line.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long did you cook for the Bayonne? Huh? How long did you cook for them?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: We cooked for over 40 days. The government paid us for a little over 40 days for our service for the firefighters.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Well, it was 40 something dollars. It was about a dollar a day I think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of the experience at the time? What'd you think of that?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: It was just experience. That was all. Always something new coming up. Something that you weren't expecting. I was in the morning like the fire. One morning it was so smoky outside. After a perfectly still night and then the smoke would drop down with the... I suppose it was the dampness of the evening that the smoke would drop down. And I heard noise out on the clearing near Mrs. Cockin's cabin. And I got up and Mrs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Taylor said, what are you going to do?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: And I said, I'm going to see what the racket is. And so I got up and put on my robe and went outside and took my gun. I had a revolver along with me. And I took my gun and went outside and just at the edge of the clearing on the other side was a beautiful little deer. I never had shot at a deer. I'd eaten the meat and loved it. So I thought, oh gee, that would be good to have some meat. So I took old Betsy and aimed at the deer. Well about that time the gun began to go this way and this way and this way and this way and up and down and up and down. And I got buck fever and I couldn't shoot. I couldn't shoot at that deer. I just had buck fever too bad. So I missed the deer and I went back into the cabin and stayed there. Finally got over my buck fever.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is buck fever what happens when you try to shoot a deer?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: That's when you get the shape. You get buck fever. That is your nervousness. Your entire nervousness and the nerves of your body are all out of control. And your gun arm means nothing. You just go one way or the other. I tried holding it with both hands and it didn't work or anything so I just turned around. Sam, did she ever tell you the final chapter of this Mr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Roach?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yes, I think so. When she was over on Pears. Yes, he apologized. He was sorry. I forgot.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I was going to ask you when you were on that fire cooking, did you ever have to move the camp to get away from the fire?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Not exactly. We were camped on one side of the river and they decided that that was a poor place for our camp. It was a more level place across on the other side. So they fixed up the fireplace on the other side and moved our camp over. Well, the firefighters were all out on the line and one day there was a heavy wind came up and we noticed that the men were beginning to come in off of the fire line. Both at our camp and at the other camp, the Montana camp across the line. And we wanted to know what and they said, well this is as nearly a clear place as there is from here up to where the fire line is. And they said there's no way of stopping it with this wind.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And we said, well what do we do?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: They said there's only one thing to do that we can tell. That's to take your blankets and go down to the stream. And if the fire comes close, get into the water and cover your head with a blanket, leaving an air space, and stay until the fire has passed over. It may be awfully hot, but if you're in the water you possibly could survive anyway. That would be the only way of doing it. So that's what the men, the two men that they left for Lackey Boys with us and the men that had drifted back all got their blankets and we went down to the stream. And we sat there on the log at the edge of the stream and watched the fire coming. Well somewhere, well we were sitting there, somewhere along nature took a funny little quirk and a wind began coming from the other direction and forced the fire back. And it was within a very short distance of where we were camped there at the creek. But it forced the fire back the other way and it didn't come into our little corner of the woods. So we were very fortunate that time. I thought I'd say goodbye to everybody. We did too. My father went out to Clarkia and tried his best to get McPeak or someone to give him a horse and let him go out to our cabin. He wanted to know what had happened to us. He hadn't heard from us and he didn't know what had happened to us. And he had a daughter out there somewhere and the men wouldn't give him a horse. They wouldn't allow him to leave Clarkia. He had to stay there at Clarkia and get word if he could. But he stayed there at Clarkia for a few days but they couldn't get any word from us because of the fire cut. It had cut across. When she came home she was wearing these filthy dirty pocket clothes. Her hair was singed, her eyebrows were kind of moth eaten. And her face was monster. And her revolver around her waist. She came clumping up the street. And we're glad to see her. Oh dear. Why don't you tell this man then, actually Jay Roach. You told him that I owned him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You told him that?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: About Pierce.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: About what?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: About meeting him at Pierce. Oh. She told me. I did tell you about meeting him at Pierce. But while we were on the fire line up there, actually Jay Roach was taken from the heat of the summer and all the responsibilities of the fire and the firemen and all. And he got a very severe case of diarrhea. Summer diarrhea. And he came back in the camp looking like a lost sheep. I never saw anybody look like he did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I said, what's the trouble?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: And he told me. And I said, well, I can't go myself because I have to stay and help Mrs. Taylor get something for your men to eat tonight. But if you will give me a man by the name of Ricketts, which I knew here in Moscow. I knew his wife and Jack Ricketts. I said, I'll tell him where to get some medicine in my cabin and I'll send him back to the cabin and get something which I think will help you. Well, my father being a doctor had given us all kinds of every medicine that he thought we would possibly use. And I knew where it was and what it was. And when he found Ricketts and I sent Ricketts back to the cabin. And Ricketts got some clean clothes for me and some clean clothes for Mrs. Taylor on his trip. And the medicine for A .J. Roach. Well, A .J. Roach took it and within a short time was able to get back on the firing line again and take it home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Sure were nice to him. Yeah, you were pretty nice to him considering that he wasn't very nice to you. No, he wasn't.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: But then the man was in trouble and he had to have help. And I had the means of giving it to him so I got it for him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of him though? What did I think of him?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Yes. I didn't think very much of him. But I had to respect him because he was at the end of the camp and all. And in disrespect, should not have been given to him because he was doing the job he was given to do. Although he was not trained for it. But he was doing the best he could. But I didn't like him.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: I didn't like him at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now what's your next question? If the homesteaders had been able to prove up on those places, would they have sold to the lumber company after it was proved up?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Well, the lumber companies were in this part of the country all through that section of the country. Clarkia and Meadow Country and beyond. And from there clear on up into Avery. They were buying the timber from these homesteaders. And those that were allowed to prove up sold their places. But those others lost it and lost their time and lost the money that it took to do their improving.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would there have been a pretty good profit for the people who proved up if they had been able to?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: At the time, most of the people out there would get all the way from $3 ,000 to $8 ,000 depending upon the sand of timber on the particular location. And some of them got along very nicely. This Elsie File, I just don't remember rightly whether she sold hers or whether she lost it. I think she lost it. She lost it. I think so. Mrs. Brown, Della Brown Griffith, had a claim right close to Mrs. File. And I think that she got her claim. Some of them did in that location and some didn't. Remember me telling you about dynamite being at our door. When Carter got back, he said that, no not Carter, but a man by the name of Fleming came in. Myrtle and I had been out to Moscow to see Mrs. Durham when she was in the hospital. And Carter took us out, but Carter had some other business on hand and didn't want to take us back. So my father called Avery and found that there was a man there by the name of Fleming, A .J. Fleming, from Canada that knew that section of the country and the trail and would be glad to take us back to the homestead. And that's the way we got back to the homestead was by Fleming taking us. But Fleming and dynamite over at the boys' cabin, dynamite was staying with Hansen and Larson, and they decided they wanted something to read. And they knew that Mrs. Brown had left a lot of reading material at her cabin, so they decided that they would go over and get some of this material and bring it over to us so that we'd have something to read likewise. When they went to investigate the cabin, they found phonograph records. And we had one of these little tiny ford, the first little phonograph with a long morning glory horn and all. So we had one of those, and so they saw these records over there and decided that they'd bring us some records, so they brought over a load of records for us. We had a great time playing those records we hadn't heard before. And they brought the magazines for us. And Fleming spent two nights or three nights there with us and dynamite.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he the one that liked his liquor? Was he the one that liked liquor?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Of course it was taboo for the girls. And one of these two men, when he was taking them out, back to where they were going, as they were crossing a little bridge, he looked down and said,

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Well, there's a bottle of liquor we should take while I play New And he was over a St. Patrick's Day booze up there. He'd been drunk for quite a while. But the man at the office said that he was alright and he knew the country and that he was over it sufficiently that he could take us back. Well, we picked up the bottle of liquor and he started to put it in his bag, in his knapsack, and I said, I think I'd like that. And he turned around and gave me a look and handed me the bottle. Well, I put the bottle in my knapsack then and carried it. I thought, well, you don't get any unless you take me with it. Well, we got to the cabin where the government cabin, our halfway cabin between Avery and the homestead.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was just a lean -to, wasn't it? Just bottles put up to keep the rain off you?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: There's no cabin. Oh, yes, it was just a lean -to. It wasn't a cabin, it was just a lean -to. A three -sided lean -to with a roof over it and you built your fire out in front of it and the heat come in, supposedly, to keep you warm. It did, but it also melted the snow on the roof and the water dropped down your back in the middle of the night and scared the life out of you. But, at any rate, we reached the cabin. We got the bottle an hour and a half out from the cabin.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: AJ's, oh, what have I got, a spiel with liquor caught up with him. He got to the point that he just couldn't carry his knapsack any longer. It was too heavy. So, Myrtle and I divided the knapsack and put it on our bags and helped AJ as nearly as we could until we got to the lean -to. Well, he curled up at the lean -to for a little while and we gathered up a little kindling. Everyone that was supposed to use a lean -to was supposed to leave kindling enough or wood enough for the following part to use. And we started a fire outside and got AJ next to the fire and got him sort of thawed out a little bit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I said, would you like a drink?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: And he said he would love it. So, Myrtle and I mixed up, put on some, got some hot water from the vessels there. And we fixed up a drink for each of us, making it light but with heat and the liquor was enough that Fleming soon got through enough that he could get up and get firewood enough to keep us for the night. And he set out with the fire and kept things going and we stepped inside the lean -to and fought the water dripping. But in the morning we all got up and had our breakfast and started on to the cabin. We got through the night alright. Maybe because finding that liquor was a good thing. AJ Fleming was a remittance man from Canada. He had been shipped over from England and was a remittance man from Canada and came down into that section of the country and just traveled around to get acquainted with the country. So he knew the country he was going into. In the meantime, he had gone down into Michigan somewhere. I don't know just where but Mrs. Borah and Mrs. Bush, that's Mrs. William Borah and Mrs. Bush had gone to this resort and were spending a few days at the resort. It happened to be the time that AJ Fleming came down from Canada and he got acquainted with them. So when he came in and said that he knew Mrs. Borah and he knew Mrs. Bush and it also helped to give us something that we could rely on and we felt very much better about AJ Fleming after that. He was a funny man. He wrote poetry. He could spill all poetry by the yards. I have a poem, I think I still have it in the basement, that he wrote about the homesteaders out there. He would write part of it with his left hand and part of it with his right hand. It looks so funny to see the two different handwritings on this poem.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they any good? Were the poems any good?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Oh yes, they were good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What is a remittance man? What is a remittance man?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Oh, it is a man that doesn't conform with the way of life in England and the family are practically ashamed of him. And so in order to get him out of the way, they send him over to Canada. He would pay him so much a month per year. As a British subject he would be acceptable in Canada. He was accepted in Canada. He had been in one of the wars and had a very deep scar across his forehead here and up into his hairline. We asked him once, he said, I suppose you're wondering where I got that scar and he said yes. So he told us it was during such and such a war and that he was wounded in the war but other than the scar was all that he had that showed that he wasn't. And that's why they call him a remittance man. They pay for them. The parents do. The parents or their relatives pay for them to stay out of England where they're ashamed of having them known as a cousin of so and so or the Earl's oldest brother or something like that. I wonder what you had to do to get kicked out of your own country like that. Maybe it wouldn't take too much. Maybe that scar wasn't from the war. Maybe he got into trouble. He may have had something besides a war scar. I don't know. It could have been a war scar but we had to take his word for it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, it sounds as though your father was interested in having protection for you and the other women too would rely on outside help.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But did they have to rely, would you have been able to rely on yourself in tight spots too?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: We did. We did. We hired men to come in and clear up the clearing and the buildings and put up the buildings for us. We hired a great deal of our labor from the men at Clark Hill. We had one man by the name of Carter Marsh, a Frenchman, and the other was named Al Champagne. That's a interesting name. Al Champagne was the brother of the wife of the man that owned the big saloon in Clark Hill. His name was Champagne and Al Champagne was one of them. So we had those two men. They got wood for us and Carter would get wood if we needed any. But these men and cutting down the old trees and clearing up put the wood shed full of wood so we had no trouble with the wood.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have to have a certain amount of clearing other than for protection?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Yes, you were supposed to have a certain amount of clearing and gardening and any other, what do you call it, improvements that belong on a home. I had to have a barn. I didn't have a horse, but I had to have a barn, which I used for the wood shed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the 22 months that you were there, was that in a row continuous?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Yes, with the exception of the time I took off and went to Wallace.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: One week I took off, about the first of March, and Mrs. Durham was here at the hospital at that time. Myrtle and I came out with Carter and decided to come on down to Moscow and see how the mother was getting along and to be out in civilization for a little while. And they were gardening out here over the South Hill Road. People were gardening along in there and the vegetables were coming up and you could see them. That was in March of that year. It seemed awfully good to see all this land. I know, but they're giving that land to the railroads.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would that be sort of like eminent domain, is that what they call it?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: They just go through and take property because they need it for a highway or something.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that what happened? Did the railroad just get the land?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Yes, the government just gave them certain sections of land in order to get this country opened up. And I don't know what they ever expected to do with the land. The Southern Pacific, the New York Pacific, got some wealthy. Railroad rights is what it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If your land, if your homestead had not been on the government, I mean on the railroad land, would they have left you alone?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: I presume so, but I don't know because they tackled so many of the others that... So it might have been either way. They might have gotten you one anyway. I couldn't tell how that would have been decided. I remember, I think I do correctly, you were saying something about stolen timbers. There was a discussion of that too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it a stolen timber claim or something that they wanted?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Yes, but that was down here on Ruby Creek out from Bowville.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's a stolen timber?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: It wasn't the same thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you take up two homesteads? Hm? Did you have two homesteads?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: This was a stolen timber down here. And I filed on that. You said, you were filed on that. I had the two sections.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get to prove up on the stolen timber? Huh? Did you get the stolen timber?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Yes, I did in a way. This is funny. The potlatch, I had this, it's on Ruby Creek out from Bowville. It had a mine. And Elkhorn, Bowville and Elk River on that line across there. And the potlatch lumber company were that they, no fire, so they started in the timber before a certain date in the year.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And they said, you can't start a fire, can you?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Well, some of those timbermen up there got too smart. And they started a fire in the timber, burning brush and so forth. And my father and I went out to see, someone coming in from there told us that part of my land was taken by the fire. And my father and I went out to see about it and see how much damage had been done. And the cook for the lumber company decided that we'd have dinner with them. So we had dinner with the lumbermen and the cook that day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And while we were sitting there, the old cook came in and said, you owned that timber up there?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: And I said, yes I did, at one time. And he said, the fire took it and they started the fire out of fire season. And he said, if you need a witness, I can be one for you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, you're a cook?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: He said, I'm a witness also to anything that interferes with my rights as a citizen. And he said, my rights as a citizen would be to tell you that the fire was started and that it took the timber on your claim.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And my father said, would you witness that if it was necessary?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Oh yes, he said, I'd witness it if it was necessary. So we came back to Moscow and my father and I went over to Potlatch to the main timber company office. And went in and met the president of the company there. And he wanted to know what trouble was. So we told him that it had been up to the timber and it was badly damaged. And wanted to know what could be done about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he hesitated for a little bit and he said, don't you think that a fire liability payment would be sufficient?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: And my father said yes, if it was sufficient. So we sat down and he wrote a check and I took the check and came home and cashed the check. And gave up the timber.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it a good payment?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Yes it was. It was over, well it was almost $2 ,000 for what was there. And it only cost me a very little coming and going once or twice from Bova where we spent the summers anyway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he know that you would be willing to take it to court? Did he know you would be willing to take it to court if you needed to?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: We told him afterwards that had it been necessary we would have taken it to court but we thought we'd give him a chance first. So he didn't know when he wrote you the check that you would have. Because they were just being fair. There were other people affected up in that part of the country besides my claim. And that had timber claims or sorts on Ruby Creek. I just remember that phrase, stone and timber. I never knew what it meant.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wonder what, was that about the same time do you think that the town of Boville almost burned down? Was that in that same fire?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: That was before that. That was before the town of Boville burned.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You might have owned a Ruby mine up in that Ruby Creek district wasn't it? Did you see what the land was like after it was burned? Did you see what the land was like after it had been burned over? Did you go through land that had been burned?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: It was hilly. I don't mean just your timber claim. I mean in the woods. It was what they called a stone and timber act. And you could file on it and by paying a certain amount for so many acres you could get your claim on it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What I was thinking is when you go back through timber, like through the land that had been burned over say in that 1910 fire, what did the land look like?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It does.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: If you go between here and Clarkia by way of Boville, go to Boville and then from there on across to Clarkia you pass a lot of that land which at one time had been burned over and has come back to second growth and their growth timbered. It's reforested itself. A great deal of that between Boville and what used to be Clarkia, that whole section of the country into there was lumbered off at one time, most of it, by the Potlatch Lumber Company. They were at one time called the Milwaukee Lumber Company and then after they got out west here they called it the Potlatch. I don't see how daddy ever had time to practice his medicine because he was always having someplace with her. He didn't care if he was on his daughter. Everyone was doing it. I was teaching school and had money enough to take care of these things that they came up and my father got interested in getting me connected with the pioneering out on the homesteads. I can't imagine him letting her go. So he approved of the idea. Oh yes. Because with my sister who is two years younger than myself, he was so cautious about everything we did. Maybe he learned a lesson from all the worries she caused him. I don't know. But he didn't want us to take advantage of something. I was my father's boy. Yes she was. He had no boys. He had no son. And I used to drive with him during the flu epidemic here. I would drive at night for him. He would work during the daytime at the office taking care of his patients at the office. Many a time we would drive out at night after dinner to visit the sick in the country. And Dad would be so tired that I would do the driving. I would drive to a certain point and wake him up and ask him which way to go. He would tell me where the location was. I would drive on and he would go back to sleep again. That's wonderful. That's the way. I fished with him and I hunted with him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think he just figured that you could take care of yourself?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He didn't have any real reservation about you going out on a homestead by yourself. Let me tell you something.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: She always has been able to take care of herself. Any emergency she's level headed and thinks there's some way out of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she ever tell you about the time she pulled her tooth?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: This was when she was about seven or eight years old. Tell them that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: When you pulled your tooth. I'll tell you. This was the beginning I think. She had to have a tooth pulled. And at that time it was on the dentist there. The dentist had traveled through and told Daddy what to do before he had his tooth. So I think it was Daddy that started to pull your tooth once. Yes. The dentist. I had teeth before youngsters were supposed to have teeth. They came through early some way. I don't know how. And the front teeth. And they were, I guess they were decayed. I don't know what. Because I wasn't old enough to really know. But there was a dentist came through and he, my father was telling him about the teeth. And he gave him a lot of paste and plastic. And told him what to do to save the teeth until the others were ready to come through. And so Dad came home and brought the instruments and worked on my teeth being very careful not to hurt me. And I had the, I had those teeth then until the others came through. Finally had tooth down here. It was aching. And Dad said to me, you come down to the office and I'll take care of that for you. So I went down to the drug store in the back to the office. And Dad got his faucets out and I opened my mouth and he pulled the tooth. Or part of the tooth. He just kept part of it. And before he could get the tooth out of his faucet and in the cuspidor, I was out of the chair and about three blocks down the street towards home. I went just as tight as I could go. Nobody was going to hurt a tooth of mine like that. It hurt me. And away I went. Well we had a little outdoor cellar. And above the cellar was a little open loft. And the children used to play in this loft. That was our playhouse. And we'd go up a little ladder on the outside and up. So when I went home I could feel the edge of that tooth in my tongue. And it bothered me terribly. And I went up to the little playhouse and took a shoe...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: what do you call it?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Sh... shoe... Ha ha!

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's that? What do you button your shoes?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: A shoe buttoning. Oh yeah. Yeah. Shoe buttoning. That was what we were using up there. And I took this shoe button with the hook on it. One hook. One hook. And I reached in and I pulled the rest of that tooth out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When my father came home at night he said, uh, how's your tooth?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: And I said, there isn't any tooth.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well what happened?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: I said, I pulled it. He said, you couldn't pull that tooth out. I said, I did. I opened my mouth to show him that the tooth was gone.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you do it?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: I took a shoe button. Button hook. Buttoning. And I pried it out with a shoe buttoning. I think from then on he knew she was going to do anything. He said, I could do anything. So after that... Well we came west in 18... Uh... 1993. 1893. The fall of 1993. That was the year that was so wet here that the farmers couldn't get into the fields to either cut their grain or those that had it grain couldn't get in to harvest it. And, uh,

speaker: Ione Adair
words: what is I, I started out with one particular thing to tell you about it. We came,

speaker: Ione Adair
words: now I know. We came west and Dad wanted to take care of my teeth. I had some that needed care. And she started it. And Dad said, nothing doing. Nobody gets in my mouth. So, he said, well, we'll see what we can do. We'll just let them go. So there was a dentist here in town by the name of McBride. And he had an office in the Moscow Hotel. A south office that looked directly across into the telephone building at that time. And he went to Dr. McBride and told him what the trouble was. And wanted to know if he could suggest anything. And the doctor said, I think we can.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And Dad said, oh what? And he said, uh, how would you like to come down and have dinner with us?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Bring the gal. So, my father and my mother and I went down and had dinner with the doctors. And I got quite well acquainted with the doctors. And the doctor played the violin. If there was anything I loved it was violin music. And he played for me. And we had dinner two or three times down with the doctors. And I got very well acquainted with him. And he played the violin for me. One day he said to me, I noticed when you left that you had a tooth down the ear that needs taking care of. He said, it's going to spoil your looks if you don't have it taken care of. I was quite a proud little youngster's dad. And he said, come up to the office and let me see what we can do about it. So I trudged down to the doctor's office at the Hotel Moscow. And the doctor put a big rubber band around my neck and a shield here to cover me. And worked on my tooth being very careful not to hurt me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he said, would you come back and let me do the other one?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: And I said yes. And I went back and did the other one. Well he took care of my teeth from that time on until all my back teeth came in and filled them all as they needed it. And I went to the doctor and had them taken care of. I wear the violin. I had them all taken care of. I was down at Dr. Miller's yesterday and it reminded me that Dr. Miller had the office in the telephone building which was directly opposite the windows where I used to sit at the doctor's. And I told him, I said, I years ago had my first teeth taken care of directly across here at that window on the other side. And he said, your doctor did a wonderful job. He said, your teeth are still in good condition. So I went down to Dr. Miller last night and was looking in my mouth and I said, doctor, it's been a good many years that you've glanced down into that cavern.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, are you still working it?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: He said, yes, I'm still working it. So he relined my plate yesterday so that I could get her on. So you really have a piece of candy. Try them out now. A little rest. Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Aren't they good?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: They'll stick in your teeth. They're really tasty though. I think they are. They're called orange sticks I think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Orange sticks?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: They're called Rosar's candy cutter. We'll just try them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They're good?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I think they're good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Aren't they good? Well do you think that there were people who would think that Ione's independence was too independent for a girl?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: She never worked on anything else. She never implemented it on anyone else.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well you know how people talk and all. Oh, they may have. I don't know.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Of course she was alone, there were several people from here doing approximately the same things. I think she's told you how she was one of the first people that ever wore a divided skirt. That shocked people, but then soon others began to wear divided skirts. I don't think she was too... she was courageous and I think it haunted her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And it still doesn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well some of these other women that were out there, I wonder, was it harder on some of these other ladies? What? Some of these other ladies that were homesteaders, was it harder on them than it was on you to be alone and to take that kind of life?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Mrs. Durham was a widow. She had this one daughter and she thought that would be a chance for her to make a little more than she was doing dressmaking here in town. And that was her. And Mrs. Tarson was... She married the druggist that had the store down where Birkin's Hardware is now. And she thought that it would help out a good deal if she could take a homestead when this homestead of this locator was lining up this bunch of people. She thought that if she was anywhere near someone else, it would be a good idea. So she did that. Mrs. Durham, as I said, was a widow with one daughter. Mrs. Taylor... Well, she said her husband died, but she never seemed to be very sad about it. And we thought that they probably had had a divorce. That was my idea of Mrs. Taylor at any rate. And Mrs. Brown, Dela Brown Grippus, at the time she took up her homestead, the one where A .J. Fleming and Dynamite got the books. Her brother was a locator. And she was just a young college girl at that time. Finished college. But she married Griffin after that. He was in the construction business. So we knew him and we knew her. And others out there were just about the same. Elsie Fyle was Elsie Watkins, Dr. Watkins' daughter.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: And the aunt of the young lady that wrote the buffalo coat, the history of her uncle and the buffalo coat. Were you rather close to...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: did you become rather close friends to any of these women that you were with?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: We've known them here in town. My cousin Mrs. Durham did sewing for me when we were youngsters. And for my mother. And Mrs. Torsen trimmed hats for us. And all of the rest of them. We were all acquainted one way or another. Some we knew better than others but we learned them after we got out on the homestead. This is Mrs. Durham.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was she related to the Durham and Kaufman store?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Another?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: No, she wasn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about this woman that ran the hotel at Clarkia?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: That woman that you mentioned to me. Mrs. Ferdy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was she French?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Ferdy F -E -R -T -I -G. Run it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she run a saloon there too?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: No. She had the little rooming house down the line between Clarkia and the schoolhouse. Mrs. Clark ran the two football. I remember those boys. Clark.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They ran the, what do you call them, sir?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Collins too. We called her Aunt Clark. Yes, she ran a rooming house at Clarkia. I mean at Bowbelt.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you think of yourself at the time as being a pioneer? To think of myself? As a pioneer?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Oh no. I never thought about it being a pioneer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you know, it seems as though you're a... Nowadays, when women will, it seems, will do a lot more, have a lot more independence than they used to. It seems like you had that independence back then. The sort of thing that a lot of women nowadays are striving for.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: You see, my father was born in Iowa. And his father was a doctor and went up into Colorado. He got the mining fever and went up to Colorado, one of those big mines up around Boulder, and Colorado Springs were opened up. French Creek, I think they called it then. And left my father and his wife with a sister of hers by the name of Taylor in Iowa. The Taylors moved out to Oregon, down at Amity, Oregon, out from Portland. And my father was just a youngster when they took him out there. So he was raised in the West and in that open country down around Portland. And so when he landed here, it was still West to him after having been in school. Because his father died. Our father's father was dead or died when he was up in this mining area with his men. He went out as a doctor with them. And so that left grandmother alone with two little children. The little children and grandmother, they came to Oregon. They went with the Taylors down into Oregon. That's how daddy happened to be taken to Oregon. So you figure that he was more accustomed himself to pioneering. Well, you take a youngster about 17 or 18 years old, then raised in the West and decided to become a doctor. And went back East to Rush College on his own. And worked his way through the college. And he was practically a pioneer in a good many ways. Then he came West. My mother's health was so poor in the East that he decided that he'd come West then to see the twin helpers. So he sold out his practice there in the little town. And came out to his sister's at Johnson down here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you're in Johnson, in the main town, have you been in Johnson?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: No. But I know where it is. Well, anytime you're down there on the main street, you look straight down a long lane that is from the town. And the lane used to go perfectly straight. And at the end of that lane was a great big house. And that was Uncle John's house. And so we spent our first month in Johnson. Daddy came out and we went down to Johnson to his sister's. And he took mother, Uncle John took mother and daddy down to Pullman. And to decide whether they wanted to locate in Pullman or in Moscow. You told me about that. I think I told you about that. Because she didn't like the hills. That's right. She didn't like being out of town. There were no memories then. She didn't want the hills there at all. So we went, came into Moscow. And I'm glad because I don't like Poland. She said she would take Moscow because Third Street was the only hill then that she could see in Moscow, except in the mountains. Was your mother's family, she grew up on a farm.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was her family a poor family? Was her family a poor family when she was a girl as farmers? Or were they fairly well fixed?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well they farmed.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Well, he had a farm. And they made very good work on the farm. And that was, they were not wealthy. No farmer is wealthy. Them. But they made their living by living on the farm. And they all died early. I think farming must be very hard. I never had any grandparents because they were all dead before I was born.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you know, this, I wondered what you think about this with nowadays with women striving to be independent. You know, trying to be independent and have jobs and work of their own and not just be housewives and that kind of thing. And I was wondering, I mean this seems like what you were doing back then without even thinking about it.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Indiana was one very heavily populated at that time. Her sister nature, I think. The sister next to her wasn't of that temperament at all. She liked to flirt with the boys. She didn't flirt. Not yourself. But she was an adventuresome person. She never would have cared to have gone on none of the things that I understand. Do you think that... She liked challenges.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And she didn't, you think she would see them as difficult or hard going?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Something new.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: I think the only thing that puts the fear of God in her is electricity. She's afraid of electricity. Electric storms she doesn't like. And anything in the house that looks like it might be throwing a little spark into bringing me to get an electrician out. Because she just will not touch it. So that's how she's afraid of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I still don't know if she understands just what I mean when I try to say it to her. But compared to the way that people are today, it seems like she was a pioneer in more ways than one. Yes she was.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Although they didn't call her that. I mean she was like some of these women that started out... Well I think Abigail Adams was really one of the first women that worked for women's rights. Although she didn't call it that, but she started that sort of thing. And I think just without realizing, this has always gone her own way. She'll even... I won't say she'll argue with the doctor, but if she doesn't pick a sense of it, she will pick such a sense.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well back in those days when she was young, was she interested in women's rights at all?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't think so. She had too many others. But women had to go here pretty early.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: She was mixed up in politics just once, unless when she was elected county treasurer and tax collector. And she went into civil service at the post office. But now she liked going out and giving these speeches for electioneering for her office. Daddy went with her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't know how many of you have done that, because he was always looking out for Iowa.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: And she really didn't need looking out for us much because she was so independent.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: He was that way with all the sources, as I said. Was there much of a campaign to run for... Oh I don't think so. They had to make all these surrounding towns and give talks. Old beating.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you tell me once that when your father was really quite sick that he had you come and make him up? Or his eyebrows?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: I was struck by that. Yeah. He was always in bed just before his death. I took care of him for six months. Which I love. I wanted to be a doctor. But I was born in the wrong generation before... Oh, she said she was proud. I'm glad to hear her say that to me. But when his beard began to turn gray, his hair never turned gray. But his eyebrows and his beard were kind of reddish, yet again getting gray speckles in them. His eyebrows got gray. So one day, when he was still quite comfortable in bed, he asked me to go up to the office and get a small bottle of bottle or something. So I went and got it. Not knowing what he would give it. We said there was a toothbrush pad. So I brought it up and he put this on his eyebrows, not his beard. And he was still in bed, but he still was proud. I just loved him. His periods.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you think of that professor's name? Which one?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: One that talked about Idaho history. Oh, I had been trying to...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Brosnan?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: It was Brosnan.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Brosnan?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Yes. Brosnan. Brosnan.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How do you know about Brosnan?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, well I just knew that he taught Idaho history here.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: My father... I think you told him this too. Did I tell you about the...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Daddy and his hearing aid?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: That's a good last one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Aren't they good little things?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: And, well, my father got a... was getting a little bit deaf and it bothered him. With his profession he had to be able to hear what everyone said. So we got him a hearing aid. It was one of the very first ones that were put out. And the hearing... the battery split into his vest pocket. And the hearing aid came up into his ears. Well, he wasn't sure he was getting good service out of that. And he would, at home, go into one room and then ask us to speak to him. And then he'd try to catch what we said. And he kept on with that, but trying a little lever, high and low and medium and so forth. And on it. Well, Daddy belonged to the Chamber of Commerce. And one day Dr. Brosnan was the speaker at the Chamber of Commerce on Idaho history. He was especially interested in the spring up here on Paradise Ridge. And this monument that's out here where the old... Well,

speaker: Ione Adair
words: it's on B Street, A Street, I guess. Out at the north end there's a big rock. There's a monument out there. And he was telling about those things. And my father decided that he would go down and listen to the Idaho history by Dr. Brosnan. So he took a place quite close to Dr. Brosnan and went in to take out his hearing aid. And Dr. Brosnan would go on with his story. And they had to sit here and he'd shift the lever one way or he'd shift the lever the next way. Dr. Brosnan would lower his voice and he'd shift it again. Well, he watched Dr. Brosnan to see what he was going to say or how he was going to speak in order to change his hearing aid. And he watched him intently. When he came home and we said, did you hear Dr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Brosnan?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: He said, no, I couldn't understand what he was saying. I said, well, that's just too bad. You'd have to try again. So the next day I was up at the post office at the university station which was then...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do they call it now?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: The U -Lut. And Dr. Brosnan came through the office and stopped to talk to me. And he said, you know, Miss Adair, I didn't know that your father was so interested in Idaho history. And I said, oh yes he is. I said, he's quite a western man in the beginning and is very interested in anything that is historical. Well, he said, you know, I gave a speech at the Chamber of Commerce yesterday and there wasn't a man at that table that gave me the attention your father did. I just thought that. I didn't have the courage to tell him that my father came home and said he hadn't heard a word.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, isn't that good?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: That's a good story.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have some special things you wanted to ask her about, Sam?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wanted to ask her about teaching a little bit too. About what the experience of teaching was like. Now, you told me once when I asked you about teaching, about the time that the supervisor came out and you had to bring the class back. But I was thinking about more of what teaching was like at that time.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: I can't tell you what teaching was like. What about in the public schools, I know. You had to do several grades. The public schools are so changed now. In the early days, I had usually high third and low fourth grades and had all the way from forty to fifty youngsters in the two grades in a room. And you carry on your discipline with that many children and your instructions and all for that many children and you've got your hands full. And now they think if they had twenty or possibly twenty -five, they have a full room and they have no time for preparation excepting after hours. And in the early days, you did your work yourself and you did it on your own time. I can remember her coming home with stacks of papers as she was grading and eating them. Had written lessons. And they were disciplined too. The children, when I was in school, it was about the time she was teaching. You had to march in and march out of the school rooms. You were never allowed to just visit the door freely. You marched from the walk up to the school and took your seats. Once I was kept in school, a whole week, an hour after school every night because I chewed gum and cut it. They were all together different things. You were responsible for the children coming and going to school. If anything happened, you were responsible for them. And so you had your discipline not only at the school but either coming or going. Well, you couldn't be everywhere at once. No, but it was your responsibility. No, but somebody would tell on them. You'd always remember. Always somebody to tell, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It reminds me at the time that Harry Orchard was in the, what was it, the WIP, what did they call that? The IWW?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: The W -Union. Before that I think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The what? What was it, where? What about Harry Orchard? What about Harry Orchard? Harry Orchard? Did you ever hear about Harry Orchard? I've heard about him, but what were you going to say about him?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Oh, he was instrumental in killing some men down around Boise. He was in the Union and they were having this fight for Union wages and he was instrumental in killing them. And I was teaching over here at what was then the old Russell School, the building where this new building is now. And had the fourth grade and a little youngster in it with a name of Horton McCauley. His sister was the speech director at the university at that time and Horton was in my room. And there were things going on that we were trying to get to the bottom of it. A little group of youngsters were carrying on pretty high for a time and we were trying to get to the bottom of it. And we couldn't find anyone that would tell us that we could get to give enough of it, that we could have anything to go by. And I was talking to Horton and I said to Horton, somebody must know, somebody must tell because we have to get at the bottom of this. And he looked up and he said, Harry Orchard never confessed and I won't either. Now that was Harry Orchard. Well, it went on that way for oh, possibly a week or so. And we were still ferreting around trying to get at the bottom of this trouble. And one day the papers came out in big headlines, Harry Orchard confesses. I took the paper over to the schoolhouse with me and I showed it to Horton. And I said, Horton, you said that Harry Orchard never confessed. And I said, he did. And I said, it's up to somebody to help us out. Well, we got a full line on the whole business and it got it taken care of. I wasn't just Mr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it? What was going on? Huh? What was going on?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: The house way down at the end of F Street, the big old house still stands way down there. And there was a group of these youngsters were committing all kinds of depedations at this old house. The house was empty. It had been moved from here on A Street up to F Street where it goes up. It had been moved beyond where the schoolhouse is out there now, the other school. And we were trying to find out what was going on and who was guilty for the depedations that were being done to the house. And we couldn't find anybody.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So they were just, they were vandalizing the house?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Vandalizing the house and, oh I don't know what all was going on. All sorts of things. Well Harry Archer confessed. The little fourth grade youngsters shouldn't be doing it. But Harry Archer didn't confess. But when Harry Archer confessed we got the whole business. You'll find out about Harry Archer some of these times. Oh, I've heard about him. I've heard about him. Because I remember then they had the trial.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They had the trial of Bill Haywood and Clarence Darrow came and defended Haywood and Boro.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: And Boro, Darrow came in on that too. That was a struggle to have that large on TV. That was funny.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever meet him, Senator Bora? Did you meet Senator Bora at all? What do you remember about him?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Oh nothing. In particular he was a large man. I wouldn't say that he was over six feet tall or something like that. But he was a large man in every way. And he had a remarkable face. A face that if you saw it you remembered it. But I can't tell you what he was. The lines, they were serious and all. And yet at the same time they were not stern or forbidding or anything like that. But he was just a man that you were comfortable with at the same time. You never felt like taking any privileges or asking anything of him. He was a...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: what do you call him? A lone wolf?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well he wasn't. He was a lone ingredient. Did he talk in a common way or did he talk in a... Yes, his... Horse book.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: His social voice was very pleasing and his distinction was very good. You could understand him. I could understand him but then I did then anyway. It wasn't because I was part of him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So Sam did you take the talks that Lee gave over at the house the other day?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: He gave a talk about the museum. And Mrs. Wood had called me afterward and said she contributed some things to Lee's talk. But the things she contributed were not correct. I just wondered if you taped it and got her voice out. She told that Dr. Church came to Mother's door and asked her if she would live there. Well that was when he was going by. Mother was in the garden and they talked garden and he said this is very much like my home in Pennsylvania. And he wondered if Mother would live there. That's the way Dr. Church got into the house. And she said that he came from Indiana and knocked at the door which to me is quite different. She just... She gets off of himself. She has ideas but they're not always straight ideas.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the other thing she said that was so wrong?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: One thing that she told Lee over here was that we used to take the banisters that go up the stairs and twine the pine boughs on them for decorations at Christmas.
